item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: At least one photo in the image is copyrighted, notably the top left being a Reuters image. As such, the entire image will be taken down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP reminds me a lot of Roa from Tsukihime. Both are unfathomably old disembodied consciousnesses who reincarnate in random people across the planet, and leave a trail of death and destruction with those bodies. Just as a disclaimer, I'm not trying to accuse the author of plagiarism, but I think it's neat that similar ideas can be expressed in completely different ways and would like to provide another example of this idea for people who are interested in seeing it represented in another work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The name "Majnun" referenced by SCP-1790 has been identified by Foundation historians as referring to Qays ibn al-Mulawwah, a figure from Persian folklore who was involved in a forbidden romance with Layla, the daughter of a man who refused to allow their marriage. The degree to which SCP-1790 identifies with this figure has not been determined.

Its a very minor detail but it's driving me crazy. The famous poet Qays ibn al-Mulawwah was a real person who lived in "Najd", What is now known as Saudi Arabia.

His poems are some of the most famous early examples of "Love Poems" شعر الغزل, And students all around the Arabic world spend long dark nights trying to memorize his complex poems & verses.

He is a figure from Persian folklore as much as Romeo and Juliet are from the Egyptian folklore.

The Wikipedia Article about him is wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was just curious about the mugshots. A little research showed that 3 of them are:

-Laura Bullion, an Old West outlaw;
-Sydney Skukerman, alias Cecil Landan, an Australian conman in the 1920s;
-Laura Zúñiga, a Mexican beauty queen involved in a drug trafficking scandal about 10 years ago

So I was wondering who the 4th one is? And if it was intentional that the two females are "Laura" and Skukerman used "Landan" as an alias?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how about. we just put it within 500 meters of special containment procedures. and then see what resulting xk class kill everyone scenario plays out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a pity there are no available containment cells on the Moon. That seems comfortably outside of the reincarnation radius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably the most monstrous SCP I've read yet. No voting however since it's just a reincarnating sociopath and feels like a thriller-fantasy movie villain.

item.title: Re: If they want to get rid of it...
item.content:encoded:
Keter

Such subjects must be cataloged, contained according to special containment procedures, and destroyed, if possible.

I mean, yeah,

Cases where Keter-class objects that can be neutralized by Foundation personnel persist in Foundation custody are rare,

But,

and are grouped into three main categories. In addition to cases where destruction of the object continues to be unfeasible due to apparent invulnerability or similar circumstance, there are some Keter-class objects with significant tactical value for the Foundation, as well as Keter-class objects that threaten more harm to humanity from their neutralization than from their continued existence.

And this scp does none of those 3 things.

I don't know why some people think that the SCP foundation is some sort of museum for doomsday devices. It's not containment for the sake of containment, it's containment for the sake of safety. So you should probably make this euclid.

item.title: Re: If they want to get rid of it...
item.content:encoded:
It has been well established that Keter class SCP objects should, whenever possible, be destroyed.

No.

The only Keter being actively worked against is 682. Even Keters can provide good research, like many other SCP items.

item.title: Re: If they want to get rid of it...
item.content:encoded: It has been well established that Keter class SCP objects should, whenever possible, be destroyed. At lest that has been my interpretation of part of the definition of Keter, that the ongoing containment research is at least partially aimed at the decommissioning of said threat. Particularly when a aspect of its danger is the inability to fully and consistently contain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… It's The Master, except with long range regeneration? No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like it, I just wish I could identify any of those people in the picture. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked for procedure Antipas, that was pretty cool. Otherwise a pretty average SCP. +1

item.title: Re: If they want to get rid of it...
item.content:encoded:
You think there are that many newborns in a 500 mile radius of complete vacumn?
It's a solid plan, the problem is that it is counter to SCP Foundation mandate.
They don't want to perma-kill him. There are so many options that might work very well if that was their goal (including launching him into the sun). But the Foundation would rather keep him in a box forever, a much more difficult proposition.

item.title: Re: If they want to get rid of it...
item.content:encoded: Then it would die and take over a new body?

item.title: If they want to get rid of it...
item.content:encoded: Launch it into space. They have ways to stick her on the moon, or at a lagrange point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Antipas" is a reference to Herod Antipas, whose father supposedly did some very naughty things to babies once. I was thinking about adding a description of the procedure in the addendum, but i'll leave it as is for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It says in the article that cognition continues, so I think this would work, except for putting two SCPs together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a Trill without the slug or the cheetah spots?

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very cool. I like that it's persuasive powers are entirely natural; it just knows people because it's been around that long.

I'm not up on Latin much, so I just wanted to quip that "Antipas" is really close to "Antipasto" and I can't help but laugh at this. The presentation of the procedure is up there with 110-Montauk, though, don't get me wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No upvote because it just doesn't grab me, but kudos for "Procedure Antipas."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1790 is to be blindfolded at all times, and fitted with vocal restraints whenever it is outside its cell,

SCP-1790's persuasive abilities are maximized when it is able to converse with another person face-to-face; covering SCP-1790's eyes,

I don't see why she needs to be blindfolded while inside her containment cell, unless it's the difficulty of getting a blindfold on her without making eye contact. And if she was blindfolded while in her cell, she could take it off unless her hands were bound behind her back, or unless it was a specially made blindfold with connected parts that locked around the neck and back of the head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The difficulty there is that the newborn baby has to be born at pretty much the exact same time as the current host dies, and that would be pretty complicated for the Foundation to completely control - we'd have to keep ready-to-pop women hostage constantly and regulate air/naval traffic through wherever the isolated containment region would be. That's what I was alluding to at the end with the request from Dr. Samesh - he'd like to implement something like that, but the Ethics Committee isn't quite comfortable with the logistics of putting it into operation.

We don't know what happens if there's no newborn available at time of death - maybe it dies permanently, maybe it takes over an adult body, maybe something even worse - and considering how dangerous this person can be outside containment, we don't want to find out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, there was an episode of 7 Days (or how it was called in english - a series about an agent with limited-temporal displacement machine sent to the past after major fuckups) where there was an insane murderer who did exactly this.
Still I rather like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems to me to not be beyond reasonable to find a place where only a small amount of newborns would be available for reincarnation. Regardless, I love the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was well written and interesting, so it got a +1 from me, but I do feel it might be better contained in a place with no newborns for 500 kilometers (just stick in in some desert or arctic tundra facility), and therefore Euclid might have been enough. It's still very good anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: reincarnatory, genocidal, charismatic sociopath that's aware of the foundation and is impossible to permanently contain, and is actively improving its ability to avoid capture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not quite feeling the Keter on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of a recent Beavis and Butthead cartoon where a cult thought Beavis was their reincarnated leader. :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't familiar with that SCP before I wrote this one, but I imagine that the state induced by sticking them in there would be close enough to brain death that it would induce transmission to a new baby.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reincarnating cult leader, I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Solution: Stuff the guy into SCP-762 and leave him/her/it to rot (metaphoricly speaking).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but it's not my favorite of yours. The cultists is centuries, maybe millennia old, gathering all of the knowledge and wisdom of dozens of lifetimes of experience, and it still has the mindset of a Bond villain. "Oh, you wily British bastards, you may have stopped my evil plans this time, but you will not stop me for long! Let me explain in detail why I am an inhuman freak of biology and theology, and detail for you approximately what I had planned on doing! Let me do this repeatedly, in every new iteration of my existence, until somebody inevitably connects all of the dots together and learns how to contain me! (evil laughter)"

I do like it, though not enough for an upvote. It just seems really first-season X-Files to me.
